name: Stage 2: La Mongie to Soulom — Grand Raid des Pyrénées
name_original: Étape 2 : De La Mongie à Soulom — Grand Raid des Pyrénées en off
description:
To start this second stage, you will benefit from a slightly descending false flat of a little less than three kilometers as a warm-up. You then begin a long portion of ascent of eight kilometers with 1,200 meters of elevation gain. This takes you to the top of the emblematic Pic du Midi de Bigorre with its observatory and restaurant. Savor the exceptional panorama it offers over a good part of the Pyrenees chain.

The descent is made by turning back to Lake Oncet, then you continue towards Lake Bleu de Lesponne. Between the two bodies of water, the course oscillates without however taking the side of one or the other. A true Pyrenean jewel, the Lac Bleu de Lesponne surprises as much by its beauty as by the view it reveals over the valley below.

A short climb leads you to the Col de Bareilles and its striking panoramas. You then descend to discover Lake Ourrec and its pretty pastoral plateaus. Then you tackle the last real climb of the day, which includes 200 meters of elevation gain. A false flat descending for several kilometers leads you gradually to Hautacam.

From the ski resort, you still have about ten kilometers of descent to the end point of this stage. It is the small village of Soulom that reaches out to you. Attached to that of Pierrefitte-Nestalas, it offers all the amenities and therefore represents a good opportunity to refuel.
region_intro: Jogging in Pyrénées offers access to a diverse mountain landscape characterized by rugged peaks, lush valleys, and numerous pristine lakes. The region features varied terrains, from technical mountain trails with significant elevation changes to more accessible paths through forests and along rivers. Extensive networks of well-maintained trails provide numerous options for running enthusiasts. Best jogging routes in Pyrénées The most popular jogging route is Stage 1: Vielle-Aure to La Mongie — Grand Raid des Pyrénées, a 19.2 miles (30.9 km) difficult trail that takes 5 hours 40 minutes to complete. This route is part of a renowned ultra-trail race, featuring substantial elevation gain. Another top favourite among local runners is Trail at the summit of Montagnon, a difficult 9.5 miles (15.2 km) path. This trail leads to a summit, offering panoramic mountain views. Local runners also love the Col de Bastanet and Pic de Bastan Trail, a 10.3 miles (16.5 km) trail leading through high-altitude terrain with views of lakes and the Néouvielle range, often completed in about 2 hours 37 minutes. Jogging in Pyrénées is defined by rugged peaks, lush valleys, and pristine lakes. The extensive trail network offers options for different ability levels, from challenging mountain ascents to more gentle valley runs. The routes in Pyrénées are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 250 reviews. More than 21,000 runners have used komoot to explore Pyrénées's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in the Pyrénées?
A: The Pyrénées offer a vast network for runners, with over 680 routes available on komoot. These range from easy jogs to challenging trail runs, catering to all experience levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on running trails in the Pyrénées?
A: Running in the Pyrénées offers incredibly diverse terrain. You'll find everything from technical mountain trails with significant elevation changes to more accessible paths through lush valleys, forests, and along rivers. The region features rugged peaks, glacial cirques, and pristine lakes, providing a varied and inspiring landscape for every run.

Q: Are there running routes suitable for beginners in the Pyrénées?
A: Yes, while many routes are challenging, there are options for beginners. Komoot lists over 20 easy running routes in the Pyrénées, perfect for those looking for a more gentle introduction to the region's beautiful landscapes.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in the Pyrénées?
A: The running routes in the Pyrénées are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 250 reviews. More than 21,000 runners have explored the varied terrain, often praising the breathtaking scenery, well-maintained trails, and the sense of adventure the mountains provide.

Q: Can I find running routes that pass by waterfalls or lakes in the Pyrénées?
A: Absolutely. The Pyrénées are dotted with countless waterfalls and high-altitude glacial lakes. Notable features include the Grande Cascade de Gavarnie within the &lt;a href="https://tourism-occitania.co.uk/home/discover/occitania-in-a-nutshell/the-pyrenees/landscapes-of-the-pyrenees" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cirque de Gavarnie&lt;/a&gt;, the 'Cola de Caballo' (Horsetail waterfall) in Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, and the picturesque Ibones (mountain lakes) of Anayet. Many running paths offer stunning views of these natural wonders, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e880496677/col-de-bastanet-and-pic-de-bastan-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col de Bastanet and Pic de Bastan Trail&lt;/a&gt; which provides views of multiple lakes and the Néouvielle range.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the running trails?
A: The Pyrénées are rich in landmarks and viewpoints. You can find routes that offer panoramic vistas from peaks like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5448276" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pic du Midi de Bigorre&lt;/a&gt;, or traverse mountain passes such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/128884" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Col d'Aspin&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features unique geological formations like the Mallos de Riglos. Many trails lead to summits, providing incredible views, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e878756180/trail-at-the-summit-of-montagnon" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Trail at the summit of Montagnon&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there options for public transport to access running trails in the Pyrénées?
A: While the Pyrénées have an extensive trail network, public transport access can vary depending on the specific starting point of a route. Some larger towns and valleys may have bus services, but for more remote trailheads, personal transport is often necessary. It's advisable to research local transport options for your chosen route in advance.

Q: Are there running routes that are family-friendly?
A: Yes, the Pyrénées offer routes suitable for families, particularly those with less elevation gain and through more sheltered valleys. While specific family-friendly routes are not highlighted in the top selections, the region's diverse terrain includes many accessible paths through forests and along rivers that are perfect for a family jog or walk.

Q: What is the best time of year for running in the Pyrénées?
A: The best time for running in the Pyrénées generally depends on the altitude and desired challenge. Summer (June to September) offers the most favorable conditions for high-mountain trails, with warmer weather and less snow. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures for lower-altitude routes, with stunning flora in spring and vibrant colors in autumn. Winter running is possible but often requires specialized gear and awareness of snow and ice conditions, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any circular running routes in the Pyrénées?
A: Yes, many running routes in the Pyrénées are designed as circular trails, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. This is a popular option for runners who prefer not to arrange transport at the end of their run. You can filter for circular routes on komoot to find options that suit your preferences.

Q: Can I find running routes that are part of famous races or long-distance trails?
A: Yes, the Pyrénées are home to renowned long-distance routes and ultra-trail races. For example, sections of the Grand Raid des Pyrénées are popular running trails, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1030612598/stage-1-vielle-aure-to-la-mongie-grand-raid-des-pyrenees" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Stage 1: Vielle-Aure to La Mongie&lt;/a&gt;. These routes offer a chance to experience the challenging and iconic paths used by elite trail runners.

Q: Are there places to stay or eat near the running trails?
A: The Pyrénées region has numerous villages and towns that offer accommodation and dining options. Along many trails, especially in higher altitudes, you'll find mountain huts and refuges. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2830945" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estom Refuge&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2829825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bastan Refuge&lt;/a&gt; are examples of places where you might find facilities. It's always a good idea to check availability and opening times, especially outside peak season.
tips:
The Pic du Midi de Bigorre is a peak in the French Pyrenees culminating at 2,876 meters above sea level.

Open all year round, its 600 square meter terrace offers an exceptional panorama of the Pyrenees chain. There is also an astronomical observatory, a television relay and a restaurant.

For more information :
https://picdumidi.com/fr/prepare-your-stay/practical-infos
Located at 1,947 meters above sea level, the Bleu de Lesponne lake is one of the most beautiful in the Pyrenees. This natural lake has been raised slightly to regulate the flow of the Adour.
The Col de Bareilles peaks at 2,238 meters above sea level. This passage offers a superb view of the Lac Bleu below as well as the Pic du Midi de Bigorre.
Lake Ourrec is a small high altitude lake in the Pyrenees located at 1,667 meters above sea level. Nestled in the intimacy of the Lesponne valley, its banks lined with meadows offer an idyllic break.
Soulom is a small village in the Hautes-Pyrénées located on the Gave de Pau. Authentic and preserved, it has a few accommodation options.
